data.title: Omnicell: Facing New Headwinds
data.description:
Today, we are going to put Omnicell, Inc. (NASDAQ:OMCL), a small cap medical technology solutions provider in the spotlight for the first time. The company just posted its Q1 results on Tuesday. Despite top and bottom line "beats" by the quarterly numbers, the stock fell over 15% in trading on Tuesday. Buy the dip or avoid the "falling knife" scenario? An analysis follows below.

Omnicell, Inc. is headquartered out of Fort Worth, TX. The company provides medication management solutions and adherence tools for healthcare systems and pharmacies both in the United States and globally. The company has two main revenue streams: Products & Services. The services revenue is recurring and consist of SAAS and Technical Services.

The shares trade just north of $25.50 a share and sport an approximate market capitalization of $1.2 billion.

1st Quarter Results:

Omnicell posted its Q1 numbers on May 6th before the opening bell. The company posted non-GAAP profits of 26 cents a share, six cents a share above expectations. Non-GAAP income came in at $12 million, compared to just $1 million or 3 cents a share in the same period a year ago. Non-GAAP EBITDA increased to $24 million versus $11 million. On a GAAP basis, Omnicell had a net loss of $7 million compared to a GAAP net loss of $16 million to in 1Q2024. Non-GAAP gross margins fell 230bps year-over-year to 42.1%. Management blamed the decline on lower product volumes and seasonal expenses.

Revenues rose 9.6% from the same period a year ago to just under $269.7 million, more than $9 million north of the consensus estimate. This was up $24 million from the same period a year ago. Revenues were down $37 million sequentially from the fourth quarter of 2024 due to seasonality. Product Revenue was $145 million, and Services revenue was $125 million.

Despite beating both on top and bottom-line expectations, the stock fell just over 15% Tuesday. Part of this was due to management stating the company will see some tariff impacts. Leadership outlined a $40 million non-GAAP EBITDA impact from tariffs for 2025 and also stated it would mitigate some of these impacts by near shoring and optimizing its supply chain.

Leadership provided the following updated guidance for both Q2 and FY2025. The two big changes from previous guidance issued after fourth quarter results is that FY2025 non-GAAP EBITDA is now predicted to be between $100 million to $145 million range compared to $140 million to $155 million. In addition, non GAAP EPS guidance for the fiscal year has been reduced from $1.65 to $1.85 a share to $1.00 to $1.65 a share. This is a significant drop.

Analyst Commentary & Balance Sheet:

The analyst firm community is somewhat negative on the company's prospects. Following Q4 results, four analyst firms including in Wells Fargo and JP Morgan maintained Hold ratings, including two with downward price target revisions. Both Piper Sandler ($69 price target) and Benchmark Co. ($62 price target) reissued Buy ratings. Since first quarter results hit the wires yesterday, Craig-Hallum reissued its Buy rating.

Approximately five percent of the float outstanding in the shares is currently held short. The CEO of the company bought nearly $950,000 worth of shares in mid-March. That is the only insider activity in the shares so far in 2025. Omnicell ended the first quarter with $387 million of cash and marketable securities on its balance sheet. Total debt was listed at $341 million. The company produced $10 million in free cash flow in Q1.

Conclusion:

Omnicell made $1.71 a share (non-GAAP) on $1.11 billion in revenues in FY2024. The current analyst firm consensus has profits rising slightly to $1.77 a share as revenues increase barely to $1.13 billion. They project earnings to come in at $1.95 a share in FY2026 on four to five percent revenue growth. Given management's guidance on Tuesday, profit projections for FY2025 if not FY2026 will be taken down significantly.

That leaves the stock trading at 15 times trailing earnings and 1.1 times revenues. The shares do not pay a dividend. While those valuations are a significant discount to the S&P 500 (SP500), they are not compelling. Profits will drop this year, and there is no quarterly dividend payouts. While management took a first swipe at impacts from tariffs, these are initial guesses and there is no certainty on what final tariff policies will eventually be given ongoing trade talks.

Therefore, I am passing on any investment recommendation on Omnicell at this time. Hopefully, by the time second quarter results come out, more clarity will be available around the lay of the land. Both around tariffs and the global economy and their impacts on Omnicell from a longer-term perspective.

data.title: Coupang: Better Than Amazon
data.description:
Coupang (NYSE:CPNG) reported robust earnings for the first quarter thanks to growing e-Commerce volumes and customer growth on its platform. The South Korean e-Commerce business benefited from double-digit top-line growth as well as an improving EBITDA trajectory (including higher EBITDA margins). With growth in all major key financial metrics taking place, and shares of Coupang still being cheap given its growth potential, I continue to see deep value in an investment in the e-Commerce platform. Coupang’s shares have also marginally outperformed Amazon's (AMZN) so far in 2025, and I believe the South Korean company has a ton of potential to grow its earnings going forward.

Previous rating

I previously rated the e-Commerce platform a strong buy – An E-Commerce Bargain – because I liked Coupang’s revenue and free cash flow growth that was the result of aggressive platform investments in the past. Coupang continued to convince on a whole slate of financial metrics in Q1’25, including operating income and customer growth, which creates a strong revaluation upside case for the e-Commerce platform.

Coupang met EPS estimates for Q1’25

The South Korean e-Commerce company met EPS expectations for Q1'25, but missed on the top line: Coupang reported $0.05 per-share in adjusted EPS, and reported $7.9B in revenue, missing the forecast by about $110M.

Coupang generated a total top line of $7.9B in Q1’25, showing 11% year-over-year growth while Product commerce revenues reached $6.9B, showing 6% year-over-year growth. Coupang’s growth was mainly driven by the core products segment and a higher customer base. In total, the South Korean e-Commerce company grew its customers 9% year-over-year to 23.4M, which is providing fuel for future platform growth. As customers also tend to spend more money over time on Coupang, customer growth will remain key for Coupang to grow both its EBITDA and operating income going forward.

In terms of EBITDA (margins), Coupang performed well in the first fiscal quarter as well. Coupang’s EBITDA reached $382M, showing 36% year-over-year growth. The company's EBITDA margin at the same time improved from 3.9% to 4.8% during the last four quarters, highlighting a margin improvement of 0.9 PP.

Coupang remained widely profitable in the first quarter, and the company has made strides in improving its operating income as well. In Q1’25, Coupang generated $154M in operating income, showing 285% year-over-year growth.

Stock buybacks now set to ramp up...

Coupang announced a new $1.0B stock buyback authorization that will allow the company to repurchase its Class A common stock in the market going forward. Stock buybacks have not yet played a major role in the company's capital allocation policy, but the fact that Coupang is now focusing on stock buyback is a good sign for investors that the company will return more excess free cash flow to shareholders in 2025.

Coupang’s valuation

Coupang is undervalued, in my opinion, with a price-to-earnings ratio of 34.3X, which is significantly below the longer-term, 3-year average price-to-earnings ratio. The reason for this is that Coupang only recently turned profitable, and the e-Commerce company is now laser-focused on improving its platform profitability and driving earnings growth. Coupang could also acquire other, smaller e-Commerce platforms, potentially even outside of South Korea in other Asian markets, which could help the company accelerate its top-line growth while adding new revenue streams to its business.

For comparison, Amazon, America’s largest online retailer, is trading at a price-to-earnings ratio of 25.4X... which compares to a 3-year average P/E ratio of 30.2X. Therefore, Amazon is trading at a 16% discount to its historical valuation average.

I believe Coupang could trade at a P/E ratio much higher going forward, under the condition that the company continues to grow its operating income and EBITDA. Coupang is expected to grow its earnings by a massive 45% this year and 116% next year, so the e-Commerce company is going to see a nice acceleration in its EPS growth. Amazon, on the other hand, is projected to grow at much slower rates of 12% and 17% in FY 2025 and FY 2026, so Coupang is set to massively outperform its bigger American e-Commerce rival.

Risks with Coupang

Coupang is focused on growing in the South Korean e-Commerce market, which is a relatively small market compared to the U.S. or China. I see a huge opportunity for Coupang to grow in emerging markets, and missing out on this opportunity may actually be the company's biggest risk. Additionally, e-Commerce companies face unique challenges stemming from the U.S. announcing new tariffs, which could negatively affect e-Commerce volumes globally going forward. What would change my mind about Coupang is if the e-Commerce enterprise were to see slowing EBITDA and operating income growth in FY 2025 and beyond.

Final thoughts

Coupang submitted a strong earnings sheet for the first fiscal quarter of FY 2025 on Tuesday, which resulted in a solid top-line beat and resulted in shares of the e-Commerce company revaluing 6% higher so far. Coupang is growing its product revenue at double digits, and the e-Commerce firm is succeeding in growing its EBITDA margins, a key performance metric for e-Commerce platforms.

Customers continue to flock to the Coupang e-Commerce business, which is a crucial driver of the company's top-line growth. While Coupang has not seen as large a valuation draw-down as Amazon this year, the South Korean company’s earnings potential seems underpriced to me, given that it is expected to dramatically outperform Amazon in terms of EPS growth. With Coupang also having an opportunity to potentially extend its tentacles to other markets in Asia in order to accelerate its growth, I believe a buy rating remains justified.

data.title: EVgo: Finally Charging Ahead (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
EVgo, Inc. (NASDAQ:EVGO) is soaring on a solid earnings report in a signal the business has officially turned around. The EV charging station company is finally producing solid utilization numbers to warrant more interest in the stock. My investment thesis is more Neutral on the stock following the improving financials, but still offset by massive capex costs ahead.

Higher Utilization

EVgo reported a solid quarter with revenues finally reaccelerating to new record highs as follows:

The EV charging station company reported sales jumped nearly 37% to reach $75 million. The key to the story is that charging network revenue grew 49% YoY to reach $47 million.

The biggest issues facing the charging station companies since going public was the actual lack of charging network related revenues. Most of the public companies were obtaining product sales at very low margins, with limited recurring or repeat revenues from actually charging EVs.

Even worse, the charging network utilization rates were extremely low while the companies were rushing to build new charging stations. EVgo only had a public network daily utilization rate of 9% back in Q1 2023 and the number has now jumped to 24%. The company has 32% of charging stalls with a utilization rate above 30% as the business finally reaches levels of a healthy business.

In fact, the public companies originally didn't even report any data on utilization rates, probably due to the rates being so small. The whole focus was on building new charging stations with multiple stalls, which would drive topline growth, but also drove much higher costs as well.

EVgo is now reporting a 37% gross margin on the charging network revenue, versus overall gross margins in the 10% range just last year. The end result is an adjusted EBITDA loss of only $6 million and forecasts for possibly breaking even for the year.

The overall growth rate was only in the 37% range due to the extent of product revenues having slower growth. The ultimate forecast should definitely shift to the charging network revenue growth with quarterly revenue set to top $50 million with EVgo adding 1,200 to 1,400 charging stalls this year:

  • Q1 2025 - $47,098 (49% YoY)
  • Q4 2024 - $46,513 (73% YoY)
  • Q3 2024 - $43,052 (98% YoY)
  • Q2 2024 - $36,444 (146% YoY)
  • Q1 2024 - $32,354 (153% YoY)
  • Q4 2023 - $28,335 (212% YoY)

The one major negative is that the sequential growth in Q1 nearly stalled versus the prior March quarter.

Big Plans

The stock appears to have set a bottom above $2 with the big 32% rally following Q1 2025 earnings. EVgo has a market cap of only $1.1 billion, with guidance for 2025 revenues reaching $340 to $380 million.

The company forecasts the DoE loan provides the opportunity to triple charging stalls to 11,000. At this network size, EVgo forecasts revenue reaching upwards of $1.1 billion with the charging network gross profits of close to $600 million driving an adjusted EBITDA of $400+ million.

The key to the story is the adjusted G&A expenses won't even grow 2x to ~$169 to $195 million. EVgo ended Q1 with just over $31 million in adjusted G&A for 41.6% of revenue and an annual run rate of $124 million.

The company will utilize the $1.25 billion DoE loan in order to complete the network build out, so EVgo will have in excess of $1 billion in net debt when the ~7,500 charging stalls are complete. The company just obtained the 2nd tranche of the loan in the amount of $19 million, pushing the debt position to $96 million with a current cash balance of $171 million to end the March quarter.

Accounting for that, the stock would have an EV of $2 billion range with an adjusted EBITDA of goal of upwards of $400 million. The stock currently trades at 5x future EV/EBITDA goals.

An investor can start believing in these big plans with EVgo wiping out the losses and having access to cheap capital to build out the charging network. The company still isn't out of the woods, with the ongoing small EBITDA losses and larger operating losses when adding in depreciation charges.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that EVgo has made a solid turnaround. The EV charging station company has far more positive financials and is on the verge of producing positive EBITDA, but the network charging revenue sequential growth has nearly stalled right before the company turns positive.

Investors can definitely make a speculative bet on EVgo here, but our view still needs to see the business grow at scale from here.

data.title: Palantir: Expensive Stock For All Good Reasons (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Palantir Technologies Inc. (NASDAQ:PLTR) reported its first quarter earnings on the 5th of May, beating estimates on revenues and earnings per share in line with expectations. The stock has suffered a double-digit loss in response to earnings, but has been ups 13.5% since my bearish call in February 2025.

I believe that the stock price has significantly improved on signs that President Trump will request a $1 trillion security budget. It does not remove my view that Palantir is overvalued, but it does paint an outlook for the company that is better supported by the defense budgets.

Palantir Exceeds Expectations On Revenue Growth

Total revenues increased by 39% to $884 million beating analyst estimates by 2.5% or $21.7 million. Government revenues increased by 45% to $487 million while commercial revenues increased 33% to $397 million. This brought the government share in sales to 55% with the remaining 45% being commercial sales. This is consistent with our sales mix seen in 2024. U.S. commercial revenues increased 71% to $255 million accounting for 64% of the commercial sales. U.S. Government sales increased 45% to $373 million bringing the U.S. share in government sales to 76.6%. Total U.S. sales accounted for 71% of all sales. International commercial sales were down 5% year-on-year and 11% due to headwinds in Europe.

Operating income grew 73% to $391 million on margins expansion of eight percentage points to 44%. Adjusted free cash flow increased from $149 million to $370 million with better cash conversion.

During the quarter, customer count rose 39% year-on-year and 8% sequentially to 769 with commercial customer count rising 46% year-on-year and 9% year-on-year. The total remaining performance obligation stood at $1.90 billion with $905 million in bookings.

Palantir Confirms Full Year Outlook

For the second quarter, the company expects $936 million in revenues at the mid-point and $401 million to $405 million in adjusted operating income. This compares favorably to analyst expectations of $932.4 million. Prior to the first quarter earnings release, analysts expected revenues of $899.1 million for the second quarter. So, the revenue outlook is strong.

For the full year, the company expects around $3.9 billion in sales compared to analyst estimates of $3.87 billion. These estimates have increased around 3% over the past month and 11.4% over the past six months. Given that the guidance exceeds the revenue estimates from estimates, I believe this shows a more positive 2025 than expected by analysts. Adjusted income has been guided for at $1.717 billion at the mid-point.

While the guidance exceed analyst estimates, they are a reconfirmation of the company’s guidance provided during the fourth quarter earnings presentation. As a result, the guidance is unlikely to have investors much and perhaps the fact that the guidance was not lifted was one of the reasons the stock sold off.

PLTR Stock Provides A Speculative Buy Opportunity

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

I previously had a sell rating on Palantir, and if we compare the stock price against the fundamentally supported stock prices using a peer group valuation, Palantir is not cheap by any means. The stock is overvalued despite a >30% annual growth expected for EBITDA and free cash flow.

At the same time, Palantir is a big data specialist and if we look at leveraging data analytics to increase efficiency and save money then Palantir is positioned extremely well. If we look at governments, especially the U.S. Government or commercial customers at this point nearly everyone is looking at leveraging data to reduce costs. I would almost say that Palantir is at the forefront of that with a moat in national security and defense applications. At a current EV/EBITDA of 190.5x, Palantir is expensive and its own EV/EBITDA median is negative. To value the stock using the company’s EV/EBITDA, I have discounted the current EV/EBITDA by 50% and see what that provides in terms of price targets. It shows that below $93, the stock is a strong buy and given the growth ahead for now a buy rating rolls out with 14%.

It puts Palantir on the list as an investment opportunity if you are willing to accept that the stock is trading far above its fundamentally supported stock prices and if you believe in the need for data analytics ingestion to drive down costs and advancing intelligence and defense solutions, Palantir stock is a Buy. I previously had a Sell rating, and I maintain my view that Palantir stock is overvalued against any fundamental metric. However, I do believe that its product can provide critical roles that will enhance efficiency and safety and eventually drive value to shareholders.

Conclusion: Palantir Is For The Speculative Buyer With Vision On The Future

I believe that Palantir is expensive, but for all good reasons. What we see quite often with companies that do something big and are going through their growth phase is that the stock price is way above fundamentally supported levels and there are a lot of investors that do not believe in the valuation or investment story. I could not say they are wrong, but I do believe that current high valuation standards also do not imply that the stock is set for failure. I believe it is not the case. So, while I do view the stock as extremely expensive at current levels I do provide a buy rating as I do believe that the product offering is what is going to justify higher price levels not only by 2027 but moreover in the years beyond that.

I also believe that the prior headwind of lower defense budgets causing share price pressure for companies with exposure to national security solutions is dissolving. The U.S. is heading towards a $1 trillion defense budget, budgets in Europe are rising and as I am writing this report a new war between India and Pakistan has just started. The geopolitical space is getting increasingly more complex by the day it seems and Palantir provides a potentially pivoting piece of the puzzle.

data.title: KMLM: Adaptive Shifts Amid Currency And Commodity Volatility
data.description:
Index and Sector Performance

The KFA MLM Index, which is tracked by the Mount Lucas Managed Futures Index Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:KMLM), finished the quarter down 2.6%. Global Fixed Income (+0.2%) markets contributed to performance while Currency (-2.5%) and Commodity (-1.3%) markets detracted from results. Interest income added 1.03%. For the twelve months ending March 2025, the Index was down 6.8%. Year to date, the Index is down 2.6%.

Index and Sector Exposures

Over the course of the quarter, Commodity exposures went from net short 41% to net short 4%, Currency exposures went from net short 92% to net short 43%, and Global Fixed Income exposures went from net short 42% to net short 69%.

Outlook

Much has changed in the market since the turn of the new year. Post-election, the market priced in what it believed to be a growth-oriented administration. Equity markets finished near highs, the Dollar strengthened, and bond markets declined as previously priced rate cuts were unwound. Post-inauguration has been a different story, with the market no longer just pricing in the positive effects of a growth-oriented Trump administration but starting to come to terms with the negative shocks of DOGE and tariff saber rattling.

At first, markets did their best, trying to forecast potential scenarios and assign outcomes based on zero-sum scenarios. US equities fell, European equities rallied. U.S. bonds rose in price, European Bonds fell. The US was fiscally retreating, with Europe and China fiscally expanding. The Dollar fell while other currencies rallied. As we write these notes, the market has shifted from pricing zero-sum outcomes to all negative outcomes after Liberation Day. Reversal risk via tweet abounds.

Managed Futures strategies historically have provided diversification in times of market stress, but to date, have only sidestepped the worst of it. Strategies with allocations to equities have fared worse. Uncorrelated, yes, but just not getting the negative correlation as markets (particularly bonds and currencies) have whipsawed over the last few months. Those negative correlations typically come with sustained price moves as markets move to new levels to reflect changing economic realities and the day-to-day noise subsides.

In the Commodity sector, the top three contributors were Gold, Natural Gas, and Live Cattle, while the bottom three detractors were Crude Oil, Heating Oil, and Unleaded Gas. In the grain markets, prices rallied in February, but by the end of March had given up that gain and ended flat or lower. Wheat and Soybeans held their short positions for small gains, but Corn got long in February, only to unwind that position and start building a short at the end of the quarter for a loss. In metals, Gold and Copper rallied sharply through the quarter. Gold entered long and profited nicely, while Copper started short and picked up the long mid-quarter for a small loss. In the energy sector, a pre-inauguration rally in Crude Oil, Heating Oil, and Unleaded Gas prices dragged the Index into long positions, only for prices to reverse sharply into quarter-end. Natural Gas continued higher, getting long early and contributing positively to results. In agriculture, Sugar started the year lower, then rallied higher and went through multiple price swings, ending the quarter with a long position. Live Cattle started the year long and followed prices higher for a nice gain. Major shifts in exposure during the quarter included Copper (short to long), Corn (short to long to small short), Natural Gas (short to long), Sugar (small short to small long).

In the Currency sector, the top detractors were the Japanese Yen, Euro Currency, and British Pound, with only the Canadian Dollar eking out a small gain. At the start of the year, short-term positions in currencies reflected the recent strength in the Dollar. Ultimately, the reversal in the Dollar (weakening) led to reversals in major currencies and made it a difficult quarter for the sector. The major shifts in exposure during the quarter came in British Pounds (short to long), Euro Currency (decreased short), and Japanese Yen (decreased short).

In the Global Fixed Income sector, the top contributors were the Japanese Government Bond (JGB), Euro Bund, and UK Long Gilt. The primary detractor was the US Ultra Ten Year (TY). As the market assigned winners and losers to potential tariff threats, US bonds rallied, hurting short positions, while European and Japanese bonds continued lower, benefiting short positions. The major shifts in exposure during the quarter came in the Canadian Government Bonds (increased long), Euro Bund (long to short), and Ultra Ten Year Note (reduced short).

Net Market Exposures

Carefully consider the Funds’ investment objectives, risk factors, charges and expenses before investing. This and additional information can be found in the Funds’ full and summary prospectus, which may be obtained by visiting: www.kraneshares.com. Read the prospectus carefully before investing.

Risk Disclosures:

Investing involves risk, including possible loss of principal. There can be no assurance that a Fund will achieve its stated objectives. Indices are unmanaged and do not include the effect of fees. One cannot invest directly in an index.

This information should not be relied upon as research, investment advice, or a recommendation regarding any products, strategies, or any security in particular. This material is strictly for illustrative, educational, or informational purposes and is subject to change. Certain content represents an assessment of the market environment at a specific time and is not intended to be a forecast of future events or a guarantee of future results; material is as of the dates noted and is subject to change without notice.

KMLM may invest in derivatives, which are often more volatile than other investments and may magnify KMLM’s gains or losses. A derivative (i.e., futures/forward contracts, swaps, and options) is a contract that derives its value from the performance of an underlying asset. The primary risk of derivatives is that changes in the asset’s market value and the derivative may not be proportionate, and some derivatives can have the potential for unlimited losses. Derivatives are also subject to liquidity and counterparty risk. KMLM is subject to liquidity risk, meaning that certain investments may become difficult to purchase or sell at a reasonable time and price. If a transaction for these securities is large, it may not be possible to initiate, which may cause KMLM to suffer losses. Counterparty risk is the risk of loss in the event that the counterparty to an agreement fails to make required payments or otherwise comply with the terms of the derivative.

Fluctuations in currency of foreign countries may have an adverse effect to domestic currency values. The use of futures contracts is subject to special risk considerations. The primary risks associated with the use of futures contracts include: (A) an imperfect correlation between the change in market value of the reference asset and the price of the futures contract; (b) possible lack of a liquid secondary market for a futures contract and the resulting inability to close a futures contract when desired; (C) losses caused by unanticipated market movements, which are potentially unlimited; (D) the inability to predict correctly the direction of market prices, interest rates, currency exchange rates and other economic factors; and (E) if KMLM has insufficient cash, it may have to sell securities or financial instruments from its portfolio to meet daily variation margin requirements, which may lead to KMLM selling securities or financial instruments at a loss.

KMLM invests through a subsidiary, and is indirectly exposed to the risks associated with the Subsidiary’s investments. Since the Subsidiary is organized under the law of the Cayman Islands and is not registered with the SEC under the Investment Company Act of 1940, as such KMLM will not receive all of the protections offered to shareholders of registered investment companies. KMLM and the Subsidiary will be considered commodity pools upon commencement of operations, and each will be subject to regulation under the Commodity Exchange Act and CFTC rules. Commodity pools are subject to additional laws, regulations and enforcement policies, which may increase compliance costs and may affect the operations and performance of KMLM and the Subsidiary. Futures and other contracts may have to be liquidated at disadvantageous times or prices to prevent KMLM from exceeding any applicable position limits established by the CFTC. The value of a commodity-linked derivative investment typically is based upon the price movements of a physical commodity and may be affected by changes in overall market movements, volatility of the Index, changes in interest rates, or factors affecting a particular industry or commodity.

KMLM is subject to interest rate risk, which is the chance that bonds will decline in value as interest rates rise. Narrowly focused investments typically exhibit higher volatility. KMLM’s assets are expected to be concentrated in a sector, industry, market, or group of concentrations to the extent that the Underlying Index has such concentrations. The securities or futures in that concentration could react similarly to market developments. Thus, KMLM is subject to loss due to adverse occurrences that affect that concentration. KMLM is non-diversified.

ETF shares are bought and sold on an exchange at market price (not NAV) and are not individually redeemed from the Fund. However, shares may be redeemed at NAV directly by certain authorized broker-dealers (Authorized Participants) in very large creation/redemption units. The returns shown do not represent the returns you would receive if you traded shares at other times. Shares may trade at a premium or discount to their NAV in the secondary market. Brokerage commissions will reduce returns. Beginning 12/23/2020, market price returns are based on the official closing price of an ETF share or, if the official closing price isn't available, the midpoint between the national best bid and national best offer ("NBBO") as of the time the ETF calculates the current NAV per share. Prior to that date, market price returns were based on the midpoint between the Bid and Ask price. NAVs are calculated using prices as of 4:00 PM Eastern Time.

The KraneShares ETFs and KFA Funds ETFs are distributed by SEI Investments Distribution Company (SIDCO), 1 Freedom Valley Drive, Oaks, PA 19456, which is not affiliated with Krane Funds Advisors, LLC, the Investment Adviser for the Funds, or any sub-advisers for the Funds.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Uber's Q1 Miss Is My Ticket To Add - Reiterating Buy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

It's been a minute since I last covered Uber Technologies, Inc. (NYSE:UBER), which was back in February, when Uber was trading around $70 a share and I said buy. At Tuesday's close, before earnings, it was $86, up 23% since my last buy.

This morning, however, the stock took a nosedive after earnings, so investors can now pick up the stock for low $80s. Back in February, I felt that their strategy of pushing into less dense, less competitive markets was going to pay off; I anticipated them to outperform in Q1 2025. I was wrong on my Q1 expectations, but not on Uber’s longer-term growth potential. Uber missed on Q1 revenue, which caused the stock to trade 6% lower in pre-market and is now 4% lower since market open.

I'm here to tell investors they shouldn't be worried about the miss. This is because I think this was a miss instigated by high expectations, not a weaker appetite for the service Uber provides.

I still remain bullish on Uber since my last buy call at $70 per share; Investors should take advantage of this post-earnings panic around Uber and snag the stock for under $80. Remember that when Uber traded down by 8% after last quarter's report, it quickly reversed all its losses and shot up by 21% in the following days (between Feb 5th and Feb 10th). I’m expecting a similar scenario to play out this quarter, too.

Q1 2025 Earnings Breakdown

Uber reported earnings this morning, and the stock dropped; all it took was for investors to look at revenue. Uber made $11.5 billion in sales, a bit less than the consensus at $11.62 billion. Despite the miss, revenue grew 14% in 1Q25 from the same period in 2024. Earnings for the quarter were $1.78 billion, with profits of 83 cents per share. These numbers surpassed Wall Street’s expectations, which were at 51 cents per share. Again, Uber’s biggest revenue segment was mobility, bringing in $6.5 billion, down from $6.9 billion we saw last quarter. Delivery revenue was virtually unchanged since last quarter at $3.77 billion.

What investors care about, though, is gross bookings, how much Uber’s transactions are valued on their platforms. Looking into the two main segments, gross bookings for mobility were $21.18 billion, up 13% year-over-year, with delivery gross bookings at $20.38 billion, up 15% year-over-year. Total gross bookings were at $42.8 billion for the quarter, up 18% on a constant currency basis.

In terms of user growth, their monthly active customers on their platform (MAPC) is now at 170 million, this number is similar to last quarter’s MAPC of 171 million. Uber users booked in total about 3.04 billion tips on the app this quarter, which is a nice growth of 18% since this time last year. CEO Dara Khosrowshahi gives us a 2Q25 guidance of $45.75 to $47.25 billion in gross bookings, with EBITDA somewhere in the vicinity of $2.02 billion to $2.12 billion.

Management remains bullish, and so do I

For the past couple of quarters, Uber has been working pretty hard to expand both their mobility and delivery services into less dense rural areas. If you think about it, there is an endless number of rural areas out there for Uber to expand into. Management claimed on the call that “growth in mobility is being fueled by less dense areas,” and while people, there are more likely to own cars, meaning trip density is far less, they see a trend of people in rural areas being much more dependent on Uber for reserve trips. In fact, they don't mind paying the higher premium for the trip, a pretty nice tailwind going into the rural expansion in FY25.

In terms of outlook on mobility margins, management was hesitant to give away too much but did mention they expect “steady margin improvement” while trying to walk a fine balance between investments for growth and increasing profitability. I see Uber's expansion into rural areas as a catalyst for the stock, one that'll enable mobility in specific to drive profitability growth.

No one likes to pay insurance, and Uber’s no exception

Insurance prices remain the biggest headwind for Uber still and dominated the earnings call Q&A. Insurance prices in the U.S. are just too high and hurt profitability for both Uber and the driver. They're currently fighting bills in California and New York to lower premiums and requirements. One bit of good news for investors on the insurance front would be that management initially “overestimated insurance headwinds” in 2025. Initially, they anticipated insurance being high single to low teens (% of gross bookings) for 2025, but what they're seeing now is U.S. mobility insurance for 2025 being much more modest. This is nice news going into the rest of 2025.

AV is Uber's leg over Lyft

AV cars were another big talking point; management sees “more need for AV cars” and is looking to expand into Atlanta soon. Currently, they boast a 100-car AV Waymo fleet in Austin. They claim Waymo cars are “busier than 90% of drivers in Austin”; their main pain points are now safety and keeping the cars clean for the next trip. When asked about competition in the AV space, they were quick to claim no one is really doing what they're doing in terms of AV, but mentioned some companies in China are starting to utilize similar technology.

Lyft (LYFT) is probably Uber’s main rival on this front, but they’re also a distant second in making the AV cars available to customers in a meaningful way. So, this could be Uber’s next leg over Lyft heading into 2026. We should know more about where Lyft stands on all this tomorrow after their earnings report.

Valuation

Uber stock has taken a hit on earnings this morning, down from a 52-week high of $87, currently trading at a discounted $82 per share. Is this all there is for Uber, or is there still room to run between now and next quarter? I expect the former scenario to play out.

Before today, in terms of RSI, we were in overbought territory at 78; right now, the RSI has dropped to 52. Seeking Alpha puts Uber in the expensive category. Forward price to sales ratio is at 3.57 against the sector median of 1.4. The same is true of forward EV to sales at 3.57 against a median of 1.85. Uber is not cheap, but I believe it is discounted right now.

What's Next

Investors should look at this recent Uber selloff as a buy-in opportunity; Uber continues to dominate the ride-hailing industry and has a new catalyst that should help boost mobility gross bookings in the second half of the year. When it comes to delivery, a smaller but fast-growing side of their business, the company faces intense competition from DoorDash (DASH). Still, it continues to compete in what should be an expanding grocery delivery business amid macro uncertainty.

I advise investors to keep a close eye on the stock price and try and get an entry point of under $80. On the delivery front, Uber is being innovative in the space, adding new features to compete with DoorDash. They now offer the option for merchants to add real-time deals on the platform. Any seller wanting to get rid of too much daily stock might add a 2-for-1 offer or big discounts on Uber Eats. Small things like these help Uber gain the edge over competitors when growing market share.
